title: Pronunciation Practice 2: Consonants
description: Learn some of the trickiest Hindi consonant sounds designed for English speakers.

blocks.title: Tricky Consonants for English Speakers
points: Soft 't' and 'd' (त, द): Touch the tip of your tongue to the back of your top teeth. (Like Spanish/Italian).
Hard 'T' and 'D' (ट, ड): Curl your tongue back to the roof of your mouth. These are retroflex sounds.
Aspiration: Hindi distinguishes between breathy (ph, th, kh) and non-breathy (p, t, k) sounds.
Rolled 'r': The Hindi 'r' (र) is lightly rolled, similar to Spanish or Scottish 'r'.

blocks.title: Identify the Sound Difference
blocks.instruction: Listen to the difference between these pairs.

pairs.sound2: Aspirated 'ph' (like an airy p, not an 'f')
pairs.diff: Notice the puff of air in Phal.

words.note: Kh = K with a heavy breath of air. Khaa-na means food.
T = Soft t, tongue touching teeth. Tum means you.

items.statement: The Hindi 't' is exactly the same as the English 't'.
items.explanation: English 't' is alveolar (behind the teeth). Hindi has a dental 't' (on the teeth) and a retroflex 'T' (roof of the mouth).

items.statement: 'Ph' in Hindi is pronounced like an 'F'.
items.explanation: In pure Hindi, 'ph' (फ) is an aspirated 'p' (a p with a breath of air), though many modern speakers pronounce it like 'f'.
